item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that's used in this article is cc-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as it comes from here, available under CC BY 2.0.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agree with other posters, lots of good material but needs better execution, the implications of origin and reason behind its behavior could be a bit clearer

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not a fan. At all. It's really a bit on the awful side unfortunately.

Critique
Critique done

The containment procedures are still kind of excessive here - 2m containment glass? That's huge. 50m room around THAT? That's enormous, and for NO reason. The water as a way to contain the radiation and help provide light is pretty brilliant, but the extent is kind of OTT. Also, keeping lights in place is SUPER easy, and does not require -

Eight xenon short-arc lamps outside of the enclosure are to be operated during the bi-annual refill of the borosilicate enclosure. These lamps are to be replaced once yearly.

Just…keep lights on it, replace as needed. Basic maintenance.

The constant O5 inclusion here is also way overboard and unnecessary, as are the restrictions on research staff getting anywhere near it. YOU WANT THE THING RESEARCHED. If light keeps it passive, plenty of light and basic radiation gear is more than enough to do the research you need.

Stopping the testing to see what's inside it after ONE D-class died due to the radiation is…dumb. Throw a rad suit on and do it again. Problem solved.

On that note, the entire incident is kind of silly in the first place. The ONLY light in the room HAPPENS to go out during a scuffle between a D-class and an…armed security officer? Nope. Just nope. Also, that would kill the D-class, the security officer, the researcher, and at least one more person before they got it back into a passive state in that situation because it would kill all three of those people before going to find another one in a(hopefully) well-lit area.

The experiment seeing how far it can go is exactly the right kind of test the Foundation would do…but they wouldn't leave the thing inside an existing facility that they then have to clean up. They would just go and set all that stuff up in a wide open field/desert/tundra/whatever to minimize damage, because that's the smart thing to do when you're testing.

Oh, and then there's the ADDITIONAL O5 interference saying 'no more testing at all evar!' also makes no sense and is a wild over-reaction to a fairly mundane sort of hazard - we KNOW how to handle radioactive material.

tl;dr all the elements are here for a solid article, but they're used in such a haphazard, sloppy way that the end result is shockingly bad - mostly because it ignores basic common sense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked the picture, but the article itself is kinda boring. We have a lot of "kills you for no reason" skips so, downvoted. I'm sure someone could give a better use to that creepy bear.

item.title: Lel
item.content:encoded: Has no one noticed the phto of the bear is actually a teddy bear is made from a placenta? Makes this more interesting and disturbing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the concept, but the execution feels rather off. It's not precisely in danger, but it could really use a rewrite to emphasize on the good bits.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, if it wasn't for Voct's message I would have missed out the explanation of why does it do what it does, maybe I wasn't paying enough attention, or maybe it isn't very well explained in the scip itself, who knows.

The scip is rather interesting, but as some other rightfully pointed up: The "Keep some light trained at it or it will kill you dead a lot 'till you die, goddamnit!" has been used to death and more. Im upvoting just because it kind of makes sense in this particular setting.

EDIT: Also, why would you black out the date? I mean… it's pretty obvious.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmmm… Radioactive Bear that wants to be loved… Call Bruce Banner. As to what is in side… I am guessing Moshoko… Rare Substance silvery ^_^ and expressed in the way you have said.
Ether that or it is a Chunk of Anti Metal… and the casing of the Bear isn't ordinary because I doubt female insides would stop Radiation.

I would say Cut that sucker open and expend as many D's as possible till you get to see the insides.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And this is what pushed me from novote to upvote. It would be nice if this was more strongly implied in the actual article, because I considered this scip simple and boring - and too much like SCP-106, except boring - before looking at it in this light, but I can't do anything except upvote now, so I guess that's a point for Voct, if nothing else.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it. It's unnerving without being "3SPPOKYLOL" and it's slightly heartwarming in some respects. That said, it seems like a cross between 435 and 106, but the picture makes up for that by vitalizing1 it. Upvoted

Footnotes
1. I'm not sure if I used this word correctly and frankly I couldn't give a shit.

item.title: Re: Radiation units...?
item.content:encoded:
Becquerels are unimaginably tiny and you need a mega- prefix to make it mean anything at all, yes. For instance, the typical background radioactivity of a human body is 4 kilobecquerels. One becquerel = one atomic nucleus disintegrating, per second. Remember the size of Avogadro's number.

And something whose radioactivity is measured in Petabecquerels is dangerously radioactive.

item.title: Radiation units...?
item.content:encoded:
Not to bring this apparently painful topic up again, but why are the Becquerels written as PBq? Are they peta-Becquerels? I don't even know exactly how much a Becquerel is (or even if I'm spelling it right), but the peta prefix (if that is in fact what it is) probably makes it a pretty huge amount of radiation even for tiny values of a Becquerel. I also note some units prefixed with M instead; mega-Becquerels? Maybe you should write the units longhand first and then abbreviate them thereafter.

Of course I could just be a moron about the whole thing for not knowing that, say, 'Becquerels are unimaginably tiny and you need a mega- prefix at the least to make it mean anything at all', but even then it would still be nice to see the units longhand before seeing the abbreviation - just for clarity.

(I'm a total n00b so please don't eat me)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Awwww!
Poor teddy!
Just like all those zombie memes, but it's true.
I want to put on a Hazmat suit to give it the attention it deserves.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
a) you're missing the point. The radioactivity is incidental. It was a teddy bear owned by an anomalous child (or made by an anomalous artisan) in Nagasaki. When in a dark environment, the teddy bear looks for someone to hug and comfort, because everyone is afraid of the dark, right? No matter where teddy is, teddy can always find you when you need teddy. Walls mean nothing to the power of love. There may be a war going on, but nothing can hurt teddy.

and then the city was nuked, and the child was vaporized. Not only is the bear now ronin, it's dangerously radioactive.

It's not like 106. 106 wants to torment you. 1145 just wants you to cuddle it so you're not afraid of the dark.

b) I know it's just a typo, but "a radioactive ear that wants you to hug it" is a hilarious image.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As others have pointed out, this is SCP-106 but a stuffed bear. And not interesting or creepy. It's a random combination of effects. A radioactive ear that wants you to hug it and walks through walls. It's just rather boring.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's a teddy bear. It belonged to a child. The child was afraid of the dark. When it's dark, the bear looks for someone to comfort.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this, but why does lack of illumination cause the bear's effects?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I will say, the picture you found for this is sufficiently creepy to help sell me on the rest of the idea, at least on some level.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Several grammatical issues in the beginning of the containment procedures. Changing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: looks a little like it was made out of pan dulce

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think this is one of those instances where remaining faithful to something isn't the best idea. I just don't like the idea of tying something into a historical event with a fairly obvious effect. I mean, this thing shouldn't be moving around and whatnot, but of course it's going to give off radiation, it was in Nagasaki for the bomb!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah, you did it, nice job. I like this quite a bit, upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In the containment procedures it says "one two"

Notice that the one is struck through.

I tihnk personnel is a plural

Can be singular or plural

You don't measure the radioactivity of an object in Sieverts, that's how you measure the absorbed dose of a human (it has a per kilo factor, and also is a weighted dose factoring in the effect of different types of radiation whereas a scientific organisation like this would list the individual energy rates of alpha vs beta vs gamma)

I have changed measurements to Bq.

I'm a newb so maybe this is standard but why does the experiment log lead to an incident log and experiment log, also the numbers are weird?

The Incident was originally classed as an Experiment.

item.title: Re: Wow.
item.content:encoded:
Fuck yes.
This actually makes sense, unlike the OTT shit you got there.
EDIT: actually, while I still don't like it much, this'd be enough for me to remove the downvote - no longer shitty Old Man copy

item.title: Wow.
item.content:encoded:
I just realised something. The containment on this thing would be super fucking easy.

Just suspend the bear in a borosilicate glass vat filled with water. Boom, done. The water would prevent radiation from leaking out, and the Cherenkov radiation would illuminate the bear.

Fuck. Consensus, should I rewrite the containment procedures with this in mind?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Good.
I still am not a fan of this, but at least it doesn't straight piss me off.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was a re-write of an article based on a doll found in remains of the atomic detonation. As such, it was required for it to remain a faithful re-write. And who says it was recovered in 1945? It could have gone for a year or more before The Foundation was made aware of it's existence.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Measurements have been changed to Bq.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So many issues with the writing. I also take umbrage with the Nagasaki link; that just feels so cliche. IT ARE RADOACTIV BCUZ DA BOMB and you can't tell it was 1945 because it's blacked out, amirite?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You asked me on feedback on the bear when? Or specifically, did I ever OK it? Might have been that I simply wasn't there or wasn't following site 19 at the time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow, such vitriol from the fiesty lynx. I had asked for input from multiple people regarding the science of this article before I posted it. In fact, I even asked you for feedback before I posted it. Not one person said anything about my decision to use Sv rather than something else.

I'm working on converting everything to Bq now. Until then, keep your claws retracted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I used the Sv measurement as I figured that would be the easiest for non-nuclear folks to understand. If the consensus believes I should be using a different unit of measurement, I'll consider changing it.

It makes no fucking sense.
A Sievert is a measure of energy received from radiation per kilogram of living tissue, scaled by type of radiation.
This thing is emitting radiation.
You are either a) abusing the unit to mean energy emitted per unit mass, which is not what Sievert means, and as such won't mean anything for non-nuclear folks either.
b) using Sievert correctly, which leads to absurd conclusions like that when someone will enter the enclosure, they will receive a dose of radiation dependant on their mass and independant eg of the spatial angle they intersect, or distance from the radiating bear. Hell you can have two people with equal height but different height receive differing doses at the same time! Bwee!

tl;dr : Epic fail explanation.

P.S - Since when it makes sense to vote on facts?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The phosphor is on the inside, I thought I had specified that. I'll double check and fix it. I used the Sv measurement as I figured that would be the easiest for non-nuclear folks to understand. If the consensus believes I should be using a different unit of measurement, I'll consider changing it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fixed title in series page for ya.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't see why, it's incredibly destructive, predatory, and it's containment must be active to keep it contained. This is very much so like 106 in it's containment and 106 is a pretty standard ideal of Keter (hard to contain and it will fuck shit up).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There was some discrepancy in the chat about it, with some guys saying keter and others saying euclid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yes, I figured. Hence my vial suggestion (it's nowhere specified how much phosphor)
However, it won't emit light if the beta rays don't even reach it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The phosphor is supposed to have some mystical suppression effect and the fact that it emits the light needed to suppress in a different way is incidental I think.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Shouldn't this be Keter?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I just don't like this one. In essence, it's the old man, but radioactive and teddybear.

For one, the containment procedures seem needlessly extravagant…. you know that a smaller glass enclosure would probably mean less phosphorus needed to achieve the illumination desired.. besides, why not have a vial with phosphorus within 4m near it, replace it weekly, and have the thing illuminated by arc lamps on several fail-safe circuits?

SCP-1145 is radioactive, with doses measured between 300 and █████ mSv/h

No. Dose is a term and Sievert an unit relating to absorbtion of radiation. Becquerel is the unit of emission - 1 Bq = one nucleus per second decay rate.
Hell, there are other units that'd make sense ,but not what you used.

Also, where the fuck is the phosphor? On the inside of the glass block? You know that glass is enough to stop beta radiation, right? Or do the D-class have to paint it from inside?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: double post

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There are several scientific problems in the article and also poor grammar and use of words.

In the containment procedures it says "one two"

I tihnk personnel is a plural

You don't measure the radioactivity of an object in Sieverts, that's how you measure the absorbed dose of a human (it has a per kilo factor, and also is a weighted dose factoring in the effect of different types of radiation whereas a scientific organisation like this would list the individual energy rates of alpha vs beta vs gamma)

It would probably be pretty difficult to determine the decay chain (possible but you're talking about the sort of equipment at CERN I think, calorimeters), and if it's not emitting gamma, you wouldn't categorise it as a particular chain it must be something else.

xenon should be capitalised

it's should be its

I'm a newb so maybe this is standard but why does the experiment log lead to an incident log and experiment log, also the numbers are weird?

Having to keep things lit or they kill stuff has been done to death imo, worthy of a place on the list of "things wat.."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This seems like a much, much better version of the "creepy doll from Hiroshima" SCP that got deleted a few weeks ago.

Bravo, sir.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it. I wish there was a bit more background like did anyone have it before it was retrieved? How did it come to the attention of the Foundation. ect. (The above post has a nice idea.)
We should assume that all levels of radiation that come from the SCP reside within the actual bear's stuffings or insides?
Or is that normal? Either way I like it so far. The Radiation is crazy though I wonder what would happen if it came in contact with another SCP? Nice job bro.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Revised containment procedures, small changes made to description and experiment logs in order to conform to containment changes.
